eras.i: Lincoln. Empire by other names. The monetary fight Bryan lost twice to McKinley. A central bank. The 1929 crash. A New Deal that briefly held the bust in place. A postwar order built around a dollar pegged to gold.
eras.ii: After the war, an intelligence apparatus designed for a global contest. Where elected governments became inconvenient, it removed them. Iran. Guatemala. Indonesia. Chile. The body count from this period runs into the millions. By 1975 the public read enough of the agencies' own files to ask what else.
eras.iii: The dollar comes off gold. Reagan rolls back the postwar policy stack. September 2001 reopens the playbook for foreign wars. The bailouts that follow run the opposite direction of the New Deal's.
eras.iv: Citizens United legalizes the money. Snowden documents the mass collection. The Epstein files surface and disappear. Gaza is funded, armed, and shielded. Palantir, OpenAI, and the data-center race come for the rest.
eras.v: Eras I through IV document the systems. Era V documents the people running them — named, on the record, with primary sources and witnesses.

anchors.y1898: The Spanish-American War. By 1898 the United States had taken Cuba, Puerto Rico, Guam, the Philippines, and Hawaii. A continental republic acquired an overseas empire in a single year, and built the institutions to administer it.
anchors.y1900-mckinley: 1900. The election that decided the U.S. monetary architecture — gold standard, tariffs, overseas expansion — and the children's novel published the same year that scholars have since read as its allegory. Donald Trump has cited McKinley as a model since 2024.
anchors.y1913: The Federal Reserve Act creates a central banking system run by a board of governors with regional reserve banks owned by member commercial banks. The architecture mixes public and private authority over the money supply. The mechanic — that commercial banks create the money when they lend, but do not create the interest — was documented in plain language by the Bank of England in its 2014 Quarterly Bulletin.
anchors.y1929-crash: 1929. The first full demonstration of how the system behaves when a leveraged expansion reverses: collapsing prices on one side, fixed-by-contract debts on the other, and a directional transfer of real assets to whoever still has cash.
anchors.y1933: The New Deal: a policy stack that pulled measured U.S. inequality to its lowest level for thirty years. The 2008 entry below documents what happens when the same kind of crisis is handled in the opposite direction.
anchors.y1944: Forty-four allied nations meet at a New Hampshire resort and design the postwar monetary order around a U.S. dollar pegged to gold. The IMF and the World Bank are created here. The arrangement holds until Nixon ends gold convertibility in 1971.
anchors.y1945-paperclip: 1945 onward. The U.S. military intelligence program that brought more than 1,600 German scientists, including former Nazi Party and SS members, into U.S. government employment — most prominently the V-2 designer Wernher von Braun, who was relocated to Huntsville, Alabama in 1950 and went on to lead NASA's Marshall Space Flight Center.
anchors.y1947: The National Security Act creates the CIA. Fifteen years later, the Joint Chiefs would formally propose remote-controlling civilian aircraft and “orchestrating terrorism in U.S. cities” as a pretext for invading Cuba. Kennedy rejected it. The declassified document is filed under this entry.
anchors.y1948-genocide-convention: 1948. The legal architecture for what counts as genocide is written. What it leaves out — political groups — is what makes most of Era II's killings unaddressable under it.
anchors.y1953: Operation Ajax. The CIA and MI6 coordinate the overthrow of Iran's democratically elected prime minister, Mohammad Mosaddegh, after his government nationalizes the Anglo-Iranian Oil Company. The CIA's own internal history, released in 2013, confirms its role.
anchors.y1953-mkultra: April 1953. CIA Director Allen Dulles authorizes Project MKULTRA — a twenty-year mind-control research program whose 149 sub-projects ran human experiments, often on unwitting subjects, with LSD, hypnosis, sensory deprivation, electroshock, and other techniques. The program received its most extensive public hearing on August 3, 1977, before the Senate Select Committee on Intelligence.
anchors.y1954: Operation PBSUCCESS. The CIA-backed coup against Guatemala's elected president Jacobo Árbenz, and the thirty-six-year civil war that followed.
anchors.y1956-cointelpro: 1956 to 1971. The FBI's domestic surveillance and disruption program, targeting U.S. political organizations from the Communist Party USA to the Black Panthers, the SCLC, the women's movement, and the Klan. The November 1964 “suicide letter” the FBI mailed to Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. — published in unredacted form in 2014 — is the canonical documentary artifact.
anchors.y1964-tonkin: August 1964. The Johnson administration cites a second North Vietnamese attack on U.S. destroyers in the Gulf of Tonkin to push the Tonkin Gulf Resolution through Congress and authorize military force in Southeast Asia. A 2005 NSA declassification confirmed that the second incident did not occur.
anchors.y1965: October 1965 through March 1966. The Indonesian anti-communist purge. The U.S. embassy's role and the CIA's own 1968 assessment of the killings — declassified — are filed under this entry.
anchors.y1965-vietnam: U.S. ground combat in Vietnam, 1965–1975, plus the spillover into Cambodia and Laos — neither of them U.S. enemies on paper. Casualty figures and primary sources are below.
anchors.y1973: September 11, 1973. The first 9/11 the United States is involved in.
anchors.y1975: The Church Committee. A year of Senate hearings on the conduct of the CIA, FBI, NSA, and IRS — and the largest single declassification of intelligence-agency conduct in U.S. history.
anchors.y1975-condor: Operation Condor. The Southern Cone military regimes' coordinated cross-border campaign of state terror, run on infrastructure the United States helped supply.
anchors.y1971: Nixon closes the gold window. The Bretton Woods arrangement ends; the dollar becomes a fiat currency.
anchors.y1980: Reagan rewires the postwar economic compact. From this point onward, productivity and the pay of the typical worker — which had moved together since the war — separate. The Central America policy is documented in the next entry.
anchors.y1980-el-salvador: El Salvador's civil war, 1980–1992. The U.N. Truth Commission's findings and the U.S. role in training the death-squad battalions are documented below.
anchors.y1998-iraq-sanctions: 1998. Two senior U.N. coordinators resign, in succession, over the U.N. Security Council's sanctions regime on Iraq. Both publicly call it genocide.
anchors.y2001-attack: An event still being argued about. The records below cover the verifiable threads from on and around the day itself — questions about timing, physical evidence, and who knew what when. Silverstein's lease, signed weeks before. Zim's lease, ended weeks before. The Dancing Israelis. The gold in the WTC 4 vault. The records that burned in WTC 7. The passport in the rubble. Hanjour's piloting reports. The Odigo instant messages. The Jennings lobby account. None of these prove anything on their own. They are the questions, with citations.
anchors.y2001-aftermath: A CIA officer goes on television in 2007 to confirm the agency tortured. He is prosecuted in 2013. The Senate publishes its own study in 2014 — 6,700 pages, of which 525 are made public.
anchors.y2002-netanyahu-iraq: September 12, 2002. Former Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu testifies before the U.S. House Committee on Government Reform on the case for invading Iraq. The premise on which he testified — that Saddam Hussein was on the verge of nuclear weapons — did not hold up in the aftermath.
anchors.y2003: The invasion of Iraq, justified by claims about weapons of mass destruction that did not exist. The intelligence apparatus that produced the case is the same one whose conduct the Senate documented in 2014. Direct and indirect casualty estimates from the war run into the hundreds of thousands.
anchors.y2008: The global financial crisis. The first GAO audit of the Federal Reserve's emergency lending, the recovery's distributional outcomes, and the foreclosure totals are documented below. Read alongside the New Deal entry above: the same kind of crisis, handled in opposite directions.
anchors.y2010: Citizens United v. FEC. The Supreme Court rules that political spending is speech and that corporations have the same speech rights as people.
anchors.y2013: Edward Snowden hands the Guardian and the Washington Post a complete archive of NSA bulk-collection programs — PRISM, Boundless Informant, Stellar Wind — and flees the country. The legal arguments the NSA had been making in secret are revealed to have been false in public. The infrastructure he documented is still in operation.
anchors.y2017-bennington: Four high-profile cultural figures — Chris Cornell (Soundgarden, May 2017), Chester Bennington (Linkin Park, July 2017), Avicii (Tim Bergling, April 2018), and Anthony Bourdain (CNN, June 2018) — die in fourteen months. Each death is individually ruled suicide. A claimed documentary tying them together — “The Silent Children” — does not appear in any verifiable production record, but the speculation it represents has circulated continuously since. What is documented is each death itself, and the FBI's own 2007 bulletin on the symbols pedophiles use to identify themselves.
anchors.y2019: Jeffrey Epstein is arrested July 6, 2019. He is found dead in his Manhattan jail cell on August 10. The two guards assigned to his floor sign false log entries. The two cameras pointed at his cell malfunction. By the next year, Ghislaine Maxwell has been arrested and convicted; many of his named clients have not. The opening poll on this page records what the public thinks happened.
anchors.y2020-pandemic-response: March 2020. The COVID-19 financial-market disruption, the Fed's three-month response, and the distributional outcomes that followed — recovered asset prices on one side, eroded real wages on the other.
anchors.y2021-surfside: 1:22 a.m., June 24, 2021. The Champlain Towers South in Surfside, Florida partially collapses. 98 dead. NIST traces the cause to the pool deck, the corroded reinforcing steel, and warnings that had been on paper since 2018. Around the same hours, separately, John McAfee dies in a Spanish prison cell. The two threads are real and documented. What ties them together, if anything, is what the rest of the page records.
anchors.y2022-eskridge: Huntsville, Alabama. The death of plasma physicist and antigravity-research startup founder Amy Eskridge in 2022 — ruled a suicide, contested in some quarters as targeted suppression — and Trump's 2025 decision to relocate U.S. Space Command headquarters to Redstone Arsenal in the same city. The institutional history of Huntsville as the postwar landing site of the German rocket scientists relocated under Operation Paperclip is the backdrop both records sit against.
anchors.y2023: October 7, 2023 begins the latest phase of the conflict in Gaza. The Max Planck Institute for Demographic Research estimates more than 100,000 conflict-related deaths in Gaza by October 2025, with life expectancy falling to nearly half what it would have been without the war. The campaign is funded, armed, and diplomatically shielded by the United States.
anchors.y2024-pagers: September 17–18, 2024. The Hezbollah pager-and-walkie-talkie operation — a supply-chain assassination at scale, since invoked any time a different device is later claimed to have done something it didn't.
anchors.y2024: Palantir's defense contracts compound. Its Maven Smart System becomes the U.S. military's targeting backbone. Microsoft, Oracle, OpenAI, and Amazon Web Services line up on the same side of the same procurement pipeline.
anchors.y2025-kirk: Charlie Kirk is shot once in the neck at a university debate in Utah, in front of 3,000 people. The accused shooter is identified and charged within 48 hours. The official ballistic, DNA, and witness record is unambiguous. Alternative explanations have circulated — including the claim that an exploding lavalier microphone, not a bullet, was the source of the wound, and assertions that other people in the field of view were involved. Each of those claims is documented under this entry alongside what the official record actually shows.
anchors.y2025: Where the page lands as of mid-2026. The threads above are presented together, with citations, so the reader can assess them.
anchors.y2021-anthropic: December 2020. The Anthropic founding split from OpenAI. The stated reason: a different model for how AI should be scaled, and how the people building it should be trusted.
anchors.y2023-altman-firing: November 17, 2023. The OpenAI board fires Sam Altman, citing a lack of consistent candor. He is reinstated five days later. The board's reasoning is what Ronan Farrow picks up two years later in the entry below.
anchors.y2026-altman: April 2026. Ronan Farrow and Andrew Marantz publish 16,000 words in the New Yorker, the product of eighteen months of reporting, dozens of named-and-on-record sources, internal memos, and a sitting senior Microsoft executive who compares Altman to Madoff and SBF.
anchors.y2026-ai-bubble: U.S. AI capital expenditure is running at roughly 5% of GDP. The historical bar at which infrastructure buildouts have busted the economy is 3%. Railroads lasted a century. Highways, fifty years. Fiber, thirty. The GPUs in this round of capex obsolesce every three to four. Daniel Priestley, who called the 2008 crash early, told Steven Bartlett what he is now saying about 2029.
anchors.y1917-balfour: November 2, 1917. The 67-word letter from British Foreign Secretary Arthur Balfour to Lord Walter Rothschild — issued during World War I, while Palestine was still Ottoman territory — committing the British Empire to facilitate a Jewish national home in Palestine. The competing British wartime promises to Arab independence and to France over the same territory are part of the same diplomatic record.
anchors.y1948-mockingbird: From the late 1940s onward. The CIA's recruitment of U.S. journalists and editors as intelligence assets and propaganda conduits — surfaced through the 1975-76 Church Committee and Carl Bernstein's October 1977 Rolling Stone reporting.
anchors.y1948-nakba: 1948. The Palestinian “catastrophe.” Approximately 750,000 Palestinian Arabs displaced and 400 to 600 villages destroyed in the war that began with the U.N. partition vote and continued through the establishment of the State of Israel.
anchors.y1967-chaos-minaret: August 1967 onward. The CIA's Operation CHAOS and the NSA's Project MINARET — twin programs targeting the U.S. anti-war movement and civil-rights leaders for surveillance, exposed by Seymour Hersh in 1974 and the Church Committee in 1975.
anchors.y1967-six-day-war: June 5-10, 1967. Israel's preemptive strike against Egypt and the war that followed — at the end of which Israel held the West Bank, Gaza, East Jerusalem, the Sinai, and the Golan, roughly tripling the territory under its control.
anchors.y1985-iran-contra: 1985 to 1986. The Reagan administration's covert sale of weapons to Iran in exchange for the release of American hostages in Lebanon — and the diversion of the proceeds to the Contras in Nicaragua, in violation of an explicit Congressional ban. Independent Counsel Lawrence Walsh's 1993 report is the canonical record.
anchors.y1985-pollard: November 1985. The arrest of U.S. naval intelligence analyst Jonathan Pollard for selling classified material to Israel. Sentenced to life in prison, paroled after thirty years, parole expired November 2020, flown to Israel December 2020 aboard a Sheldon Adelson private jet.
anchors.y1990-gladio: October 1990. Italian Prime Minister Giulio Andreotti formally acknowledges Operation Gladio — the Italian arm of a NATO-coordinated network of clandestine paramilitary “stay-behind” cells across Cold War Western Europe, elements of which had been linked to right-wing terror operations during the Italian “Years of Lead.”
anchors.y1991-casolaro: August 10, 1991. Freelance investigative journalist Danny Casolaro is found dead at the Sheraton Hotel in Martinsburg, West Virginia, on the trip he had told his brother could end with his death. The notes for the book he called The Octopus — which traced Inslaw, BCCI, the October Surprise, and Iran-Contra as a single network — were missing from the room.
anchors.y1996-clean-break: July 1996. The Institute for Advanced Strategic and Political Studies presents incoming Israeli Prime Minister Netanyahu with a strategy paper — “A Clean Break” — drafted by Richard Perle, Douglas Feith, David and Meyrav Wurmser, and others, recommending regime change in Iraq, Syria, Lebanon, and Iran. Several authors went on to senior Bush administration posts during the 2003 Iraq invasion.
anchors.y1997-dtca: August 1997. The U.S. Food and Drug Administration loosens disclosure requirements for direct-to-consumer broadcast advertising of prescription drugs. The United States and New Zealand remain the only two countries that permit such advertising aimed at consumers in mass media.
anchors.y2007-wesley-clark: March 2, 2007. Retired General Wesley Clark — former NATO Supreme Allied Commander Europe — tells Democracy Now! about a 2001 Pentagon memo describing a plan to remove the governments of seven countries in five years: Iraq, Syria, Lebanon, Libya, Somalia, Sudan, and Iran. The Iraq invasion in 2003, NATO Libya operation in 2011, Syrian civil-war involvement, and 2025 strikes on Iran have substantially intersected the list.
anchors.y2018-nation-state-law: July 19, 2018. The Knesset passes Basic Law: Israel as the Nation-State of the Jewish People. Between 2021 and 2022, B'Tselem, Human Rights Watch, and Amnesty International each independently conclude that Israeli policy in territory under its effective control meets the international legal definition of apartheid.
anchors.y1865-lincoln: April 14, 1865. Five days after Appomattox, President Lincoln is shot at Ford's Theatre by stage actor John Wilkes Booth and dies the next morning. Booth is killed at Garrett's farm eleven days later; eight co-conspirators are tried by military commission; four are hanged in July. The Deringer pistol Booth used is on display at Ford's Theatre.
anchors.y1913-frank-adl: 1913. Leo Frank — Cornell-educated factory superintendent and the sitting president of B'nai B'rith's Atlanta Gate City Lodge — is tried and convicted for the murder of thirteen-year-old Mary Phagan. In direct response to the case, B'nai B'rith founds the Anti-Defamation League in October. Frank is lynched in August 1915 after Governor Slaton commutes his death sentence to life in prison.
anchors.y1993-adl-spying: April 8, 1993. San Francisco police and the FBI raid the ADL's San Francisco and Los Angeles offices. The 400-page warrant affidavit accuses the ADL of misuse of confidential government information against more than 1,000 persons — Arab Americans, the anti-apartheid movement, the NAACP, ACT-UP, Greenpeace — gathered by ADL informant Roy Bullock and former SFPD intelligence officer Tom Gerard, and partly sold on to the South African government. No criminal charges against the ADL; a 1999 class-action settlement requires a partial records purge.
anchors.y2012-mcafee-belize: April 30, 2012. Belize's Gang Suppression Unit raids John McAfee's compound in Orange Walk, killing his dog. McAfee says it was retaliation for refusing to pay a politician; he is detained, released without charge, and flees the country seven months later after a separate murder investigation names him a person of interest. The 2021 entry below documents his death in Spanish custody and what followed in Florida the next day.
anchors.y1960-nmmp: 1960. The U.S. Navy acquires its first dolphin for hydrodynamic studies of torpedo design. By 1962 the program has become the Marine Mammal Program at Point Mugu; it has trained dolphins and sea lions for mine detection and harbor-protection missions in Vietnam, the Persian Gulf, and Iraq, and was declassified in the early 1990s.
